title: Reading and Writing Files
subtitle: Open, read, write, and close files with Python
learning_objectives: Open a file for reading with open() and the 'r' mode
Read an entire file with .read() and line by line with .readlines()
Open a file for writing with the 'w' mode and write text with .write()
Understand that 'w' mode creates the file if it does not exist and overwrites it if it does
Close a file properly with .close() to free system resources

sections.heading: Opening and Reading Files
sections.content:
Python's built-in open() function opens a file and returns a file object. The first argument is the file path and the second is the mode. Use "r" for reading (the default).

Two main methods read content:
  • .read() — returns the entire file as one big string
  • .readlines() — returns a list where each element is one line (including the newline character \n at the end)

Note for this browser environment: In this app, files exist in a special virtual folder. The files sample.txt, numbers.txt, and names.txt are already there for you — just use their names without a path.
sections.code_example:
# Read the entire file at once
f = open("sample.txt", "r")
content = f.read()
f.close()
print(content)

# Read line by line into a list
f = open("sample.txt", "r")
lines = f.readlines()
f.close()
print(lines)
sections.code_output:
Hello from sample.txt!
This is line two.
This is line three.

['Hello from sample.txt!\n', 'This is line two.\n', 'This is line three.\n']

sections.heading: Writing to Files
sections.content:
Open a file with mode "w" to write. If the file does not exist Python creates it; if it does exist the old content is completely overwritten.

Use .write(string) to write text. Unlike print(), .write() does not add a newline automatically — you must include \n yourself when you want line breaks.

Always call .close() when you are finished. Failing to close a file can cause written data to be lost (not flushed to disk) and prevents other programs from accessing the file.
sections.code_example:
# Write a new file
f = open("output.txt", "w")
f.write("First line\n")
f.write("Second line\n")
f.close()

# Now read it back to confirm
f = open("output.txt", "r")
print(f.read())
f.close()
sections.note: Mode 'a' (append) adds to the end of an existing file instead of overwriting it. You will see this in the next lesson.

sections.heading: Counting Lines in a File
sections.content: Because .readlines() returns a list, you can use len() to count how many lines are in a file. You can also loop over the list to process each line individually.
sections.code_example:
f = open("names.txt", "r")
lines = f.readlines()
f.close()

print("Number of lines:", len(lines))

for line in lines:
    # .strip() removes the trailing \n
    print(line.strip())
sections.code_output:
Number of lines: 3
Alice
Bob
Carol

exercises.title: Read sample.txt
exercises.prompt: Open sample.txt in read mode, read its entire contents into a variable called text, close the file, and print text.
exercises.starter_code: # Open sample.txt in read mode, read all its contents, close the file, and print
exercises.solution:
f = open("sample.txt", "r")
text = f.read()
f.close()
print(text)
hints: Use f.read() to read the whole file at once.
Remember to call f.close() after reading.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The file contents should be printed.

exercises.title: Count the Lines
exercises.prompt: Open names.txt, read its lines into a list called lines, close the file, then print the number of lines using len().
exercises.starter_code:
# Open names.txt, read its lines into a list, close the file
# Print the number of lines using len()
exercises.solution:
f = open("names.txt", "r")
lines = f.readlines()
f.close()
print("Lines:", len(lines))
hints: Use f.readlines() to get a list of lines.
Then use len(lines) to count them.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

exercises.title: Write Your Own File
exercises.prompt: Create a new file called my_output.txt and write two lines to it: "Hello, file!" and "Python is great.". Close the file, then open it again, read its contents, and print them.
exercises.starter_code:
# Write "Hello, file!" and "Python is great." to my_output.txt
# Then read and print the file contents
exercises.solution:
f = open("my_output.txt", "w")
f.write("Hello, file!\n")
f.write("Python is great.\n")
f.close()

f = open("my_output.txt", "r")
print(f.read())
f.close()
hints: Include \n at the end of each string you write to create a line break.
Use open("my_output.txt", "w") for writing and open("my_output.txt", "r") for reading.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The first line should be printed.
The second line should be printed.

quiz.question: What is the difference between .read() and .readlines()?
options: .read() returns a list of lines; .readlines() returns one string
.read() returns the entire file as one string; .readlines() returns a list where each element is one line
.read() only works on binary files; .readlines() works on text files
quiz.explanation: .read() gives you one big string. .readlines() gives you a list of strings, one per line, with \n characters preserved.

quiz.question: What happens to an existing file when you open it with mode 'w'?
options: New content is appended after the existing content
The existing content is completely overwritten
The file is locked and cannot be written to
quiz.explanation: Opening a file in 'w' mode always starts fresh. If the file already exists its contents are erased before writing begins. Use 'a' mode to append instead.

completion_message: Fantastic! You can now read data from files and write results back to disk. File I/O is a fundamental skill that unlocks a huge range of real-world programs.
